caveats: a: The occupation chart is NAICS 4811 scheduled air transportation rather than the six-digit passenger-only code.
a: Employment counts are not wage weights and do not reveal which tasks are already automated.
a: Exposure excludes replacing required crew or accountable safety decisions within five years.
rho: FAA evidence addresses safety assurance, not adoption rates for airline back-office tools.
rho: Smaller airlines may lack the data and integration capacity of major carriers.
rho: The estimate excludes demand, pricing and valuation effects.
e: The injected firm count is an ESTIMATE derived from size classes and an industry margin, not a verified list of 99 operators.
e: Part 121 includes cargo operators and large airlines, so certification evidence does not itself establish lens eligibility.
e: Capacity-purchase dependence may leave an otherwise eligible regional carrier commercially captive to one major airline.
s5: One large regional-airline merger is not representative of lower-middle-market firms.
s5: BizBuySell transactions are a broad small-business benchmark with no airline denominator.
s5: The range excludes internal reorganizations and asset liquidations that do not transfer a viable operator.
q: Public carrier economics skew larger than the lens firms.
q: Capacity-purchase contracts can retain savings differently from direct ticket sales.
q: This estimate addresses commercial retention only, not implementation success or traffic volume.
d5: FAA forecast scope is broader than NAICS 481111 LMM operators.
d5: Passenger miles are an imperfect proxy for a constant-quality service basket.
d5: Pandemics, fuel shocks, aircraft availability and recessions create substantial tail risk.
o: The quantity is the transportation service, not each customer-service interaction.
o: Advanced air mobility could change the supplier form beyond five years.
o: Digital channels may eliminate parts of the current service basket even while the carrier remains accountable.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Passenger carriers earn primarily ticket and ancillary-fee revenue; regional carriers may instead receive recurring capacity-purchase payments from major airlines under long-term agreements.
lens.excluded_activities: Cargo-only airlines, nonscheduled charter and air-taxi operators, captive internal flight departments, dormant certificate shells, financing vehicles, and firms outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.included_activities: US certificated scheduled passenger airlines in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band that repeatedly transport external passengers or operate scheduled regional capacity for airline customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment counts, not task-level AI exposure, for scheduled air transportation. I grouped reservations, customer service, office supervision, cargo coordination, planning and portions of maintenance diagnostics as partly exposed, while treating cockpit, cabin-safety, ramp and hands-on maintenance work as mostly non-substitutable. Because the published chart lists only the ten largest occupations and not wage-weighted task shares, the range is wider and may overstate exposure if smaller carriers have more operational labor.
caveats: The occupation chart is NAICS 4811 scheduled air transportation rather than the six-digit passenger-only code.
Employment counts are not wage weights and do not reveal which tasks are already automated.
Exposure excludes replacing required crew or accountable safety decisions within five years.
a.change_evidence: A carrier-level payroll extract mapped to O*NET tasks, with wages and current automation status for pilots, cabin crew, maintenance, operations control, revenue management, reservations and corporate functions, would materially tighten the wage-weighted share.
a.rationale: The May 2024 BLS chart shows 125,680 flight attendants, 86,450 pilots, 77,850 reservation/ticket agents, 28,520 aircraft mechanics and substantial ramp labor among the largest occupations. AI can materially reduce handling and coordination work, but the largest high-wage roles retain physical and safety-accountable duties.

d5.bridge: FAA forecasts passenger-carrier miles flown, enplanements and departures for the overall US commercial-carrier system, not constant-quality demand for LMM scheduled passenger operators. I use roughly five years of the FAA baseline passenger-carrier-miles growth near 2% annually for the base, with downside and upside spanning macro, capacity and regional-route sensitivity. This may overstate lens demand if growth concentrates at major network carriers.
caveats: FAA forecast scope is broader than NAICS 481111 LMM operators.
Passenger miles are an imperfect proxy for a constant-quality service basket.
Pandemics, fuel shocks, aircraft availability and recessions create substantial tail risk.
d5.change_evidence: A route-level five-year forecast for the exact eligible carriers, separating real passenger miles, departures, Essential Air Service support and capacity purchased by major airlines, would materially move the range.
d5.rationale: The FAA FY2026-2046 baseline projects passenger-carrier miles flown growing about 2.0% annually in the first forecast decade, consistent with a year-five quantity ratio near 1.10. Air travel is cyclical, and small regional routes can diverge sharply, so the range includes contraction.

caveats: The injected firm count is an ESTIMATE derived from size classes and an industry margin, not a verified list of 99 operators.
Part 121 includes cargo operators and large airlines, so certification evidence does not itself establish lens eligibility.
Capacity-purchase dependence may leave an otherwise eligible regional carrier commercially captive to one major airline.
e.change_evidence: An FAA/DOT certificate-by-certificate census joined to ownership, operating status, passenger revenue, capacity-purchase agreements and normalized EBITDA would identify the eligible denominator directly.
e.rationale: A genuine scheduled passenger operator serving external customers fits the recurring-service lens, but the injected 99-firm band estimate can include captive regional subsidiaries, inactive or thinly operating certificate entities, mixed passenger/cargo businesses and firms whose fleet liabilities or customer concentration make them unsuitable transferable operating companies.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 481111 ($58,190,148,799) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 481111 ($218,715,993,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1027 (margins.json[481]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Air Transport EBITDA/Sales 10.27%); revenue/employee $505,348 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 481111); class <5: 131 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,630,908 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $167,494 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 34 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,507,118 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,079,081 -> IN $1-10M band; class 10-19: 19 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,196,789 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,252,610 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 46 firms, avg revenue/firm $72,898,652 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,486,692 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 29 firms, avg revenue/firm $231,671,241 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $23,792,636 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 42 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,948,608,548 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $508,222,098 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 99

caveats: The quantity is the transportation service, not each customer-service interaction.
Advanced air mobility could change the supplier form beyond five years.
Digital channels may eliminate parts of the current service basket even while the carrier remains accountable.
o.change_evidence: FAA approvals and real passenger service for remotely piloted or autonomous transport-category aircraft, plus evidence of software platforms assuming legal operational control, would materially reduce this value.
o.rationale: Nearly all forecast scheduled passenger transport still requires a certificated accountable air carrier within five years. Digital self-service can remove booking and support contacts, but it does not replace the operator that controls aircraft, crew, maintenance and safety processes; FAA certification explicitly tests compliant, auditable safety-critical operations.

caveats: Public carrier economics skew larger than the lens firms.
Capacity-purchase contracts can retain savings differently from direct ticket sales.
This estimate addresses commercial retention only, not implementation success or traffic volume.
q.change_evidence: Carrier-level cohorts comparing implemented automation savings with subsequent route-level fares, capacity-purchase repricing, service levels and margins over multiple renewal cycles would reveal actual pass-through.
q.rationale: Airlines can retain some productivity through capacity discipline, better service and ancillary revenue, but ticket transparency, route competition, customer contract renewals and buyer bargaining pass benefits through. BTS reported a 4.5% system operating margin for 2025 and domestic fares at 70.8% of domestic operating revenue, evidence of a thin-margin, fare-sensitive model rather than durable full capture.

caveats: FAA evidence addresses safety assurance, not adoption rates for airline back-office tools.
Smaller airlines may lack the data and integration capacity of major carriers.
The estimate excludes demand, pricing and valuation effects.
rho.change_evidence: Five-year implementation roadmaps and realized labor-hour savings from at least five small or regional Part 121 carriers, separated into safety-critical and non-safety workflows, would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: Customer-service, document, scheduling and analytical copilots can be deployed within five years, but fragmented operational systems, unions, reliability requirements and certification slow implementation. FAA states that aviation ML performance must be measured within a certification framework, and Part 121 certification requires documented, auditable safety-critical processes.

caveats: One large regional-airline merger is not representative of lower-middle-market firms.
BizBuySell transactions are a broad small-business benchmark with no airline denominator.
The range excludes internal reorganizations and asset liquidations that do not transfer a viable operator.
s5.change_evidence: A five- to ten-year transaction census of US scheduled passenger certificate holders, including private sales, bankruptcies, mergers, internal reorganizations and failed processes, divided by the eligible carrier population would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Control transfers occur but are episodic and structurally difficult because certificates, fleets, labor obligations and customer agreements must survive the transaction. The Republic-Mesa combination is direct evidence that a regional airline can transfer control, while the broader small-business market recorded 9,586 transactions in 2025; neither source measures a five-year rate for LMM passenger airlines.

principal_driver: Durable need for a certificated operator combined with meaningful automation potential in reservations, operations coordination and administration.
principal_weakness: A small, hard-to-verify pool of transferable independent operators with thin margins, safety-critical complexity and strong pass-through pressure.

sources.supports: Lists the largest scheduled-air-transportation occupations and employment counts, including 125,680 flight attendants, 86,450 pilots, 77,850 reservation/ticket agents, 28,520 mechanics and operational labor categories.
sources.title: Data tables for OEWS industry charts, May 2024

sources.supports: Reports that 87% of airline pilot jobs were in scheduled air transportation and describes coordination, training and safety-accountable pilot duties.
sources.title: Airline and Commercial Pilots

sources.supports: FAA states that aviation AI/ML functionality and performance are evaluated within the certification framework to assure safety.
sources.title: Technical Discipline: Artificial Intelligence – Machine Learning

sources.supports: Explains that applicants must design, document, implement and audit safety-critical processes and comply with regulations before receiving an air-carrier certificate.
sources.title: Introduction to Part 121 Air Carrier Certification

sources.supports: States that Part 121 certificate holders include large airlines, regional carriers and cargo operators and must maintain approved hazardous-materials programs.
sources.title: Regularly Scheduled Air Carriers (Part 121)

sources.supports: Documents a 2025 regional-airline control combination and Republic's long-term capacity-purchase agreements with three major airlines.
sources.title: Regional airlines Republic Airways, Mesa Air Group are combining in an all-stock deal

sources.supports: Reports 9,586 small-business transactions in 2025 and describes the broad transaction market; used only as contextual evidence, not an airline transfer rate.
sources.title: 2025 Year in Review: BizBuySell Market Recap

sources.supports: Reports 2025 system operating margin of 4.5%; domestic revenue of $187.8 billion, with fares representing 70.8%; and labor as 37.5% of domestic operating expense.
sources.title: US Airlines profited $6.0 billion in 2025, a decrease over 2024

sources.supports: DOT final rule requires automatic, prompt refunds after qualifying cancellations or significant changes, evidencing regulated limits on retaining revenue for unprovided service.
sources.title: Refunds and Other Consumer Protections

sources.supports: Baseline forecast shows passenger-carrier miles flown growing about 2.0% annually in the first forecast decade and passenger-carrier departures about 1.6% annually.
sources.title: FAA Aerospace Forecast Fiscal Years 2026–2046

title: Scheduled Passenger Air Transportation
